era_context: 1204 was a pivotal year in medieval history. The Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople, ending the Byzantine Empire's control of its capital and fragmenting Orthodox Christendom. In the Islamic world, the Ayyubid Sultanate was consolidating power under al-Malik al-Adil. In Central Asia, Temüjin was consolidating Mongol tribal power. In Europe, King John of England was losing his continental territories to Philip II Augustus of France. In East Asia, the Jin Dynasty controlled northern China while the Song Dynasty ruled the south; Japan's Kamakura shogunate was establishing military rule.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe beyond major kingdoms

events.title: Fourth Crusade sacks Constantinople; Latin Empire established
events.description: The Fourth Crusade, originally intended to recapture Jerusalem, was diverted to Constantinople by Venetian merchants and crusader leaders. On April 13, 1204, crusader forces breached the city's walls and sacked it, massacring civilians and looting treasures. The Byzantine Emperor Alexios III Angelos fled. The crusaders established the Latin Empire, with Baldwin of Flanders crowned as the first Latin Emperor. This event marked the end of the Byzantine Empire's control of Constantinople (though Byzantine successor states continued in Nicaea, Epirus, and Trebizond), fragmented Orthodox Christianity, and demonstrated the vulnerability of the once-great Byzantine state. The sack was a catastrophic blow to Byzantine civilization and had lasting consequences for Christian unity.
key_figures: Theodore I Laskaris (future Byzantine Emperor in Nicaea)

sources.name: Geoffrey of Villehardouin, Chronicle of the Fourth Crusade
Robert of Clari, Conquest of Constantinople

events.certainty_note: The sack of Constantinople on April 13, 1204, is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the medieval period, attested in multiple contemporary sources from crusaders, Byzantines, and Venetians.
cross_references: Precedes Fourth Crusade's aftermath and Byzantine recovery (1261)

events.title: King John loses Normandy to Philip II Augustus; Angevin collapse accelerates
events.description: King John of England's military campaign to recover lost territories in France reached its nadir in 1204. After initial setbacks in 1202–1203, John's remaining forces in Normandy were defeated by Philip II Augustus's armies. The fall of Rouen (the Norman capital) in July 1204 marked the effective end of English control of Normandy. The loss of Normandy, Anjou, Maine, and other Angevin holdings was catastrophic for the English crown, shifting the balance of power decisively toward the French monarchy. This military failure intensified baronial discontent in England and set the stage for the baronial revolt and Magna Carta (1215).

sources.name: Roger of Wendover, Flowers of History

events.certainty_note: The fall of Rouen and loss of Normandy in 1204 are well-documented in both English and French sources. The date July 1204 is firmly established.
cross_references: Precedes baronial revolt and Magna Carta (1215)

events.title: Death of Minamoto Yoriie; Hojo regency consolidates in Kamakura shogunate
events.description: Minamoto Yoriie, the second shogun of the Kamakura shogunate, died in 1204 (some sources give 1199, but 1204 is more widely accepted). His death marked a turning point in the shogunate's development. The Hojo clan, who had been regents (shikken) since Yoriie's minority, consolidated their power following his death. Yoriie's younger brother Minamoto Sanetomo became the third shogun, but real power remained with the Hojo regents. This established the pattern of the Hojo regency dominating the shogunate for the next century, effectively creating a dual power structure in which the shogun was a figurehead and the Hojo regent held actual authority.
key_figures: Hojo Masako (Yoriie's mother, Hojo clan leader)

sources.name: Azuma Kagami (Mirror of the East, Kamakura period chronicle)
Heike Monogatari (Tale of the Heike, later compilation)

events.certainty_note: The date of Yoriie's death is given as 1204 in most sources, though some older sources place it in 1199. The Azuma Kagami is a near-contemporary chronicle, but it was compiled after the events it describes. The consolidation of Hojo power is well-attested.

events.title: Temüjin defeats Jamukha and consolidates Mongol confederation
events.description: Temüjin, in his campaign to unify the Mongol tribes, defeated Jamukha, a rival khan and former ally, in 1204. Jamukha had emerged as Temüjin's principal rival for supremacy among the Mongol confederation. The defeat of Jamukha removed the last major obstacle to Temüjin's unification of the Mongol tribes. This victory was a crucial step toward Temüjin's proclamation as Genghis Khan in 1206. The consolidation of Mongol power under a single leader would have profound consequences for Eurasian history.
events.certainty_note: The defeat of Jamukha is attested in multiple later sources, and 1204 is the date given in most accounts. However, the exact date is uncertain due to reliance on later compilations. The event's occurrence and significance are not disputed.

events.title: Al-Malik al-Adil consolidates Ayyubid control of Egypt and the Levant
events.description: Al-Malik al-Adil (Saphadin), brother of Saladin, had been consolidating Ayyubid power following the death of Saladin's son al-Malik al-Aziz Isa. By 1204, al-Adil had secured control of Egypt, Syria, and the Levantine territories, reunifying the fractured Ayyubid domains. This consolidation strengthened the Ayyubid state against both Crusader remnants and internal rivals, establishing a stable succession and enabling sustained military campaigns against the Crusaders in the following years.

sources.name: Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Ta'rikh)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of al-Adil's rule is well-attested in Islamic chronicles. The date 1204 marks the completion of this process.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Minamoto Yoriie's death is disputed; some sources place it in 1199 rather than 1204. The Azuma Kagami, though a valuable source, was compiled after the events and may contain errors or later interpolations. The dating of Temüjin's defeat of Jamukha is uncertain; some sources place it in 1202 or within a range of 1202–1204. The exact sequence of Temüjin's early campaigns is problematic due to reliance on later compilations. Regarding the Fourth Crusade, while the sack of Constantinople is thoroughly documented, some details (such as casualty figures and the extent of looting) are disputed or exaggerated in contemporary sources.
historiographic_note: The Fourth Crusade is exceptionally well-documented from multiple perspectives (crusader, Byzantine, Venetian), making it one of the best-attested events of the medieval period. However, crusader sources are often triumphalist, while Byzantine sources are understandably hostile. The loss of Normandy is well-documented in English and French sources, though English sources tend to blame John's incompetence while French sources emphasize Philip's military skill. Japanese sources on the Kamakura shogunate are valuable but compiled after the events; the Azuma Kagami is near-contemporary but still subject to editorial bias. Mongol sources remain problematic: all are later compilations. Islamic sources on the Ayyubids are richer but often focus on military campaigns rather than administrative detail. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Northern Europe are absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: Fourth Crusade sack of Constantinople established the Latin Empire, which fell in 1261
Sack of Constantinople fragmented the Byzantine Empire into successor states
Loss of Normandy intensified baronial discontent leading to Magna Carta
Hojo regency consolidation in 1204 established the pattern lasting until 1333
Temüjin's defeat of Jamukha enabled his proclamation as Genghis Khan in 1206
